perturbation.original_text: Such proprietary or confidential information ("Information") includes any and all technical and non-technical information, including without limitation, information concerning financial, accounting or marketing reports, business plans, analysis, forecasts, predictors, intellectual property, trade secrets and know-how disclosed in connection with the Transactions.
perturbation.changed_text: Such proprietary or confidential information ("Information") includes only specific financial statements explicitly marked as confidential and disclosed during formal presentations. It does not include business plans, analysis, forecasts, predictors, intellectual property, trade secrets, or know-how, unless explicitly stated otherwise in writing.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "Information" is significantly narrowed, potentially excluding items generally considered trade secrets under Texas law.
perturbation.law_explanation: TUTSA defines trade secrets broadly. The modified contract language unduly restricts what qualifies as "Information", potentially failing to protect legitimate trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE CHAPTER 134A. TRADE SECRETS CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE TITLE 6. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS CHAPTER 134A. TRADE SECRETS Sec. 134A.001. SHORT TITLE. This chapter may be cited as the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Added by Acts 2013, 83rd Leg., R.S., Ch. 10 (S.B. 953 ), Sec. 1, eff. September 1, 2013. Sec. 134A.002. DEFINITIONS. In this chapter: (1) "Claimant" means a party seeking to recover damages under this chapter, including a plaintiff, counterclaimant, cross-claimant, or third-party plaintiff. In an action in which a party seeks recovery of damages under this chapter on behalf of another person, "claimant" includes both that other person and the party seeking recovery of damages. (1-a) "Clear and convincing" means the measure or degree of proof that will produce in the mind of the trier of fact a firm belief or conviction as to the truth of the allegations sought to be established. (2) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, to limit use, or to prohibit discovery of a trade secret, or espionage through electronic or other means. (3) "Misappropriation" means: (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the person's knowledge of the trade secret was: (a) derived from or through a person who used improper means to acquire the trade secret; (b) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain the secrecy of or limit the use of the trade secret; or (c) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain the secrecy of or limit the use of the trade secret; or (iii) before a material change of the position of the person, knew or had reason to know that the trade secret was a trade secret and that knowledge of the trade secret had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3-a) "Owner" means, with respect to a trade secret, the person or entity in whom or in which rightful, legal, or equitable title to, or the

perturbation.justification: The original contract broadly defines "Information" to include various technical and non-technical data. The changed text significantly narrows this definition, potentially excluding items considered trade secrets under Texas law. The Law Explanation supports that TUTSA defines trade secrets broadly. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the Texas Uniform Trade Secrets Act (TUTSA), indicates the existence of legal protections for trade secrets, defining 'Misappropriation' and implying a broader scope than the revised contract's definition of 'Information'. The narrowed definition could fail to protect legitimate trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction because the revised contract's definition may not align with the statutory meaning of trade secrets under Texas law, potentially making the contract unenforceable in regards to trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Representatives means the controlled affiliates of either party, and the respective directors, officers, employees, attorneys, s and other agents and advisors of either party or of the controlled affiliates of either party. Each party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its respective Representatives and shall take all reasonably necessary measures to restrain its Representatives from unauthorized disclosure or use of information.
perturbation.changed_text: "Representatives" means only the direct, full-time employees of either party, specifically excluding attorneys, advisors, and agents. Each party shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its direct, full-time employees. No responsibility extends to the actions of attorneys, advisors, or other agents.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "Representatives" is narrowed to exclude agents and advisors, which are often critical in transactions and are often considered as representatives in a legal setting. This could limit liability for breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Agency Law - Scope of Agency
perturbation.law_explanation: Under general agency law principles, agents and advisors acting within the scope of their authority are considered representatives of the principal. By excluding them, the contract attempts to limit liability that would otherwise exist under Texas agency law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text defines 'Representatives' broadly to include controlled affiliates, directors, officers, employees, attorneys, and other agents/advisors. The changed text significantly narrows this definition to only direct, full-time employees, explicitly excluding attorneys, advisors, and agents. This contradicts general agency law principles where agents and advisors, when acting within their authority, are typically considered representatives of the principal. By excluding these parties, the contract attempts to limit liability that would otherwise exist. While the provided snippets are not directly useful, the Law Explanation clearly states this potential conflict with general agency law, and the Perturbation Explanation highlights the limitation of liability for breaches, substantiating that the change directly undermines established legal norms regarding agency and representation, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: With respect to information disclosed under this Agreement, the party to whom the information is disclosed and its Representatives shall: a. hold the information in confidence, exercising a degree of care not less than the care used by such party to protect its own proprietary or confidential information that it does not wish to disclose, and in no event less than a reasonable degree of care;
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to information disclosed under this Agreement, the party to whom the information is disclosed and its Representatives shall: a. hold the information in confidence, exercising only minimal care to prevent disclosure, provided that such care is no less than what is mandated by the receiving party's internal, written data security policies.
perturbation.explanation: The standard of care is lowered from "reasonable degree of care" to "minimal care" as defined by the receiving party's internal policies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Common Law - Duty of Confidentiality
perturbation.law_citation: Texas Pattern Jury Charges - Business, Consumer, Insurance & Employment PJC 107.1
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas law implies a duty to exercise reasonable care in maintaining confidentiality. Reducing the standard to 'minimal care' based on internal policies could violate this implied duty.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires a 'reasonable degree of care' in handling confidential information, while the changed text lowers this standard to 'minimal care' as defined by the receiving party's internal policies. This change directly contradicts the principle of maintaining a reasonable standard of care, as the receiving party's internal policies could potentially allow for a lower standard of protection than what is legally considered reasonable. The perturbation explanation confirms that the standard of care has been lowered and the law explanation indicates this might violate an implied duty to exercise reasonable care. The absence of law snippets does not change the fact that the contract has been altered in a way that goes against the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition provided: contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that the disclosing party wold be irreparably injured by a breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or its Representatives and that the disclosing party shall be entitled to equitable relief, including injunctive relief and specific performance, in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement, but shall be addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that the disclosing party *may* be irreparably injured by a breach of this Agreement by the receiving party or its Representatives and that the disclosing party *may* be entitled to equitable relief, including injunctive relief and specific performance, in the event of any breach of the provisions of this Agreement. Equitable relief is at the *sole discretion of the court*, and the disclosing party must prove irreparable harm for each instance of breach.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall" to "may" makes injunctive relief discretionary rather than presumed, and the added burden of proof contradicts the agreement's initial acknowledgement of potential irreparable harm.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code § 65.011 - Injunctive Relief for Trade Secret Misappropriation
perturbation.law_explanation: While the ultimate grant of an injunction is always at the court's discretion, Texas law recognizes the potential for irreparable harm in trade secret cases. This clause change adds a requirement to prove irreparable harm for *each* breach, potentially undermining the presumption of irreparable harm often associated with trade secret misappropriation cases, and making injunctive relief harder to obtain.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE CHAPTER 65. INJUNCTION CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE TITLE 3. EXTRAORDINARY REMEDIES CHAPTER 65. INJUNCTION SUBCHAPTER A. GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 65.001. APPLICATION OF EQUITY PRINCIPLES. The principles governing courts of equity govern injunction proceedings if not in conflict with this chapter or other law. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. Sec. 65.002. RESTRAINING ORDER OR INJUNCTION AFFECTING CUSTOMER OF FINANCIAL INSTITUTION. Service or delivery of a restraining order or injunction affecting property held by a financial institution in the name of or on behalf of a customer of the financial institution is governed by Section 59.008 , Finance Code. Added by Acts 1999, 76th Leg., ch. 344, Sec. 7.006, eff. Sept. 1, 1999. SUBCHAPTER B. AVAILABILITY OF REMEDY Sec. 65.011. GROUNDS GENERALLY. A writ of injunction may be granted if: (1) the applicant is entitled to the relief demanded and all or part of the relief requires the restraint of some act prejudicial to the applicant; (2) a party performs or is about to perform or is procuring or allowing the performance of an act relating to the subject of pending litigation, in violation of the rights of the applicant, and the act would tend to render the judgment in that litigation ineffectual; (3) the applicant is entitled to a writ of injunction under the principles of equity and the statutes of this state relating to injunctions; (4) a cloud would be placed on the title of real property being sold under an execution against a party having no interest in the real property subject to execution at the time of sale, irrespective of any remedy at law; or (5) irreparable injury to real or personal property is threatened, irrespective of any remedy at law. Acts 1985, 69th Leg., ch. 959, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1985. Amended by Acts 1987, 70th Leg., ch. 167, Sec. 3.17, eff. Sept. 1, 1987. Sec. 65.012. OPERATION OF WELL OR MINE. (a) A court may issue an injunction or temporary restraining order prohibiting subsurface drilling or mining operations only if an adjacent landowner filing an application claims that a wrongful act caused injury to his surface or improvements or loss of or injury to his minerals and if the party against whom the injunction is sought is unable to respond in damages for the resulting injuries. (b) To secure the payment of any injuries that may be sustained by the complainant as a result of subsurface drilling or mining operations, the party against whom an injunction is sought under this section shall enter into a good and sufficient bond in an amount fixed by the court hearing the application. (c) The court may appoint a trustee or receiver instead of requiring a bond if the court considers it necessary to protect the interests involved in litigation concerning an injunct

perturbation.justification: The change from "shall" to "may" weakens the guarantee of equitable relief. The original text implies an entitlement to remedies, while the changed text makes it discretionary and requires proving irreparable harm for each breach. This undermines the agreement's initial acknowledgement of potential irreparable harm. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Sec. 65.011, states that a writ of injunction 'may be granted' if the applicant is entitled to the relief demanded and some act is prejudicial to the applicant, or 'irreparable injury to real or personal property is threatened, irrespective of any remedy at law.' By adding the requirement to prove irreparable harm for each instance of breach, the modified text creates a higher burden of proof on the disclosing party, making it harder to obtain injunctive relief, which contradicts the intent of recognizing potential irreparable harm associated with trade secret misappropriation cases.
